topic: Universities should require students to take courses in climate science

systemArguments.text: Students should have academic freedom to choose their own courses based on their interests and career goals
Mandatory climate science courses add unnecessary graduation requirements and extend time to degree
Universities should focus on core disciplinary knowledge rather than ideological education
Climate science requirements impose specific political viewpoints on students from diverse backgrounds
Additional course requirements increase tuition costs and student debt burdens
Many degree programs already lack sufficient time for specialized professional training
Faculty resources should be allocated to established academic disciplines rather than trendy topics
Students can learn about environmental issues through elective courses if they choose
Mandatory courses often result in superficial learning rather than genuine understanding

goldenArguments.text: Academic freedom requires protecting students from ideological indoctrination
Educational institutions should respect intellectual diversity and viewpoint pluralism
Students have the right to pursue their chosen field without unrelated requirements
Mandatory climate courses increase educational costs and graduation time
Universities should prioritize core academic skills over political advocacy
Forced curriculum requirements reduce educational quality and student engagement
Academic resources should focus on established disciplines rather than activist education

humanRationale: System arguments show moderate sophistication in addressing academic freedom, cost concerns, and curriculum priorities. They capture several key opposition themes but sometimes conflate different issues (political vs. practical concerns). The arguments demonstrate understanding of educational policy debates but lack the philosophical depth about academic freedom and intellectual diversity found in the golden standard.
